required.title: [For Student and Mentor] The Whole-Brain Child
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

suggested.title: [For Student] The Diary of a Young Girl
suggested.author: Anne Frank

supplementary.title: [For Student] My Side of the Mountain
supplementary.author: Jean Craighead George

competence_markers.description: They can identify their primary emotional triggers and describe the pattern each one follows
They consistently separate the feeling from the action — they may feel strongly but do not act automatically from the feeling
They delay reactions in heated moments — stepping away, calling a cooling-off, choosing not to respond immediately
They manage frustration without quitting — they can be frustrated and continue working
They handle disappointment without collapsing into global conclusions about their worth or capability
They manage embarrassment without deflecting — they can own a mistake publicly without attacking someone else
They maintain an emotion journal with honest, specific entries and can identify patterns from it
They use cooling-off protocols effectively and respect them when others call them
They demonstrate growing awareness of the interpretation step — they can identify, after the fact, what story they told themselves about an event and whether that story was accurate
The weekly emotional review reveals deepening self-knowledge — the student sees patterns, not just events

narration_prompts.prompt: What are your three biggest emotional triggers?
narration_prompts.detail: How did you identify them? What pattern does each one follow?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a time you delayed your reaction to something that made you angry.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened in the delay? Did your response change from what it would have been immediately?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between the feeling and the interpretation?
narration_prompts.detail: Give an example from your own life where the same event could have been interpreted differently.
Be honest — not how you should handle it, but how you actually handle it. What pattern do you see?

narration_prompts.prompt: What has your emotion journal revealed about your patterns?
Describe a cooling-off protocol you use.
narration_prompts.detail: When did it work well? When was it hardest to use?

cross_connections.description: The self-management skills of IC1.1-B (planning, review, self-assessment) provide the operational framework for emotion journaling and pattern tracking. IC3.1-B is the emotional expression of the same self-governance capacity.
Emotional regulation under real conditions is the stress test of character. IC1 says what kind of person you are; IC3.1 reveals whether that person holds when feelings are intense.
The physical dimension of emotional regulation — breath control, heart rate awareness, physical release — is the IC3.2 partner to IC3.1's psychological work.
The interpretation step in the emotional reaction chain is where IC3.3's reframing tools are directly applied. IC3.1 identifies the pattern; IC3.3 provides the tools to change the interpretation.

Emotional regulation is the foundation of healthy relationships. Every relationship skill — communication, conflict resolution, empathy — requires the capacity to manage your own emotional state. IC3.1-B builds that capacity.
Leadership under pressure requires precisely the emotional composure IC3.1-B develops. The leader who cannot manage their own anger, frustration, or disappointment under pressure cannot lead.

Emotional manipulation is the primary mechanism of ideological capture. The student who understands their own emotional triggers and can separate feeling from action is directly resistant to manipulation through fear, outrage, and shame.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned to name your feelings, to pause before reacting, and to use words instead of outbursts. Those were the foundations — the basic tools that let you exist as a person who feels strongly and still chooses how to act. You are still using those tools. You will use them for the rest of your life.

But now the work goes deeper.

Stretching Branches was about what happens in the moment — the feeling arrives, and you manage the moment. Weathering Storms is about what happens before and after the moment. It is about understanding your own emotional patterns: what triggers you, why certain situations produce the same reaction over and over, what the feeling is actually about (which is not always what it appears to be about), and how to manage your internal state deliberately — not just reactively.

This matters because the person who only manages emotions in the moment is still at the mercy of those emotions. They survive each wave, but they never understand the ocean. The person who understands their patterns — who knows that criticism triggers shame, that disappointment triggers withdrawal, that embarrassment triggers aggression — has something more powerful than a pause. They have self-knowledge. And self-knowledge is the foundation of self-governance.

**Here is what changes in Weathering Storms:** You stop being a person who reacts to emotions and start becoming a person who understands them. You begin to see the machinery behind the feeling — the trigger, the pattern, the automatic response — and because you can see it, you can choose to respond differently. Not by suppressing the feeling. By understanding it well enough that it no longer controls you.

Most adults never develop this capacity. They spend decades repeating the same emotional patterns — the same fights, the same collapses, the same avoidance — without ever examining why. You are going to examine why. And that examination will give you a degree of freedom over your own internal life that most people never achieve.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Trigger identification** — recognizing the specific situations, people, and pressures that reliably produce strong emotional reactions
2. **Separation of feeling from action** — the feeling is not the problem; the automatic action it produces may be
3. **Delayed reaction** — increasing the gap between stimulus and response from seconds to minutes to the capacity to sleep on it before responding
4. **Management of frustration, anger, and embarrassment** — the three emotions that most commonly produce destructive automatic behavior in this age range
5. **Handling disappointment without collapse** — processing that something did not go as hoped without spiraling into conclusions about worth or capability
6. **Emotional pattern recognition** — identifying recurring patterns across situations and time
7. **Cooling-off protocols** — agreed-upon methods for pausing a heated interaction before it escalates
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Anatomy of an Emotional Reaction

Every emotional reaction has a structure. It feels instantaneous — something happens and you react — but there are actually several steps happening in rapid sequence. Understanding those steps is the first key to changing the pattern.

**The structure:**

1. **The trigger** — something happens in the external world: someone says something, a plan changes, you make a mistake, you are criticized, you lose at something, you are excluded.
2. **The interpretation** — your brain assigns meaning to what happened: "That was unfair." "They don't respect me." "I'm going to fail." "Everyone is looking at me." This happens so fast you usually do not notice it. It feels like the trigger and the feeling are the same event. They are not.
3. **The feeling** — the emotional response to your interpretation: anger, shame, fear, disappointment, embarrassment. The feeling is in your body — heat, tightness, nausea, racing heart.
4. **The impulse** — the automatic behavioral response the feeling pushes toward: yell, hit, withdraw, cry, defend, attack, run, shut down.
5. **The action** — what you actually do.

In Stretching Branches, you learned to insert a pause between step 4 and step 5 — between the impulse and the action. That was essential. Now, in Weathering Storms, you learn to see the entire chain, especially step 2 — the interpretation — which is where most of the leverage lives.

**Why the interpretation matters:** Two people can experience the same trigger and have completely different emotional responses because their interpretations are different. Your friend cancels plans. Interpretation A: "She doesn't care about me" → feeling: hurt, rejection. Interpretation B: "Something came up; we'll reschedule" → feeling: mild disappointment. Same event. Different interpretation. Completely different emotional experience.

This does not mean you can think your way out of feelings. Feelings are real regardless of interpretation. But it means that your interpretation is a variable you can examine — and sometimes change — which gives you more control over your emotional experience than you currently have.

### Part 2: Identifying Your Triggers

A trigger is a specific type of situation that reliably produces a strong emotional response in you. Not every situation — specific situations. Your triggers are not the same as everyone else's. They are yours, shaped by your personality, your experiences, and the things that matter most to you.

**Common trigger categories for your age:**

- **Criticism** — being told you did something wrong, especially in front of others
- **Exclusion** — being left out of a group, a game, a conversation
- **Unfairness** — seeing rules applied unequally, or experiencing something you perceive as unjust
- **Loss of control** — a plan changing without your input, being told to do something you did not choose
- **Comparison** — being compared to a sibling, peer, or standard and feeling like you fall short
- **Embarrassment** — making a mistake publicly, being laughed at, being caught doing something wrong
- **Feeling unheard** — speaking and being ignored, dismissed, or overridden

**The exercise:** Over the next two weeks, whenever you have a strong emotional reaction, write down: (1) what happened, (2) what you felt, and (3) how strong the feeling was on a scale of 1-10. At the end of two weeks, look for patterns. Are there categories of situations that consistently produce strong reactions? Those are your triggers.

Knowing your triggers does not make them go away. But it gives you advance warning. When you know that criticism is a trigger, you can recognize the moment it is happening and deploy your regulation tools before the wave hits full force — instead of being blindsided every time.

### Part 3: Separating Feeling from Action

In Stretching Branches, you practiced this with your body — stopping before hitting, using words instead of screaming. In Weathering Storms, the separation becomes cognitive. You learn to hold the feeling in your mind as a distinct thing — separate from any action.

**The principle:** The feeling is never the problem. Feelings are data. They are information about your state in relation to your environment. The problem is only ever the automatic action the feeling produces — when you act from the feeling without choosing to.

This means:
- You are allowed to be angry. You are not allowed to be cruel.
- You are allowed to be disappointed. You are not allowed to quit everything because of the disappointment.
- You are allowed to be embarrassed. You are not allowed to attack someone to redirect your shame.
- You are allowed to be frustrated. You are not allowed to destroy things.

**The internal dialogue:** When a feeling arrives, practice this internal statement: "I am feeling ___. That feeling is real. It does not require me to ___." Fill in the blanks honestly. "I am feeling furious. That feeling is real. It does not require me to say something I will regret." "I am feeling humiliated. That feeling is real. It does not require me to lash out."

This is harder than it sounds. The feeling will insist that the action is necessary. Anger will tell you that the attack is justified. Embarrassment will tell you that you must deflect immediately. The feeling lies about the necessity of its preferred action. Your job is to hear the lie and choose anyway.

### Part 4: Delaying Reaction — From Seconds to Hours

In Stretching Branches, the pause was measured in seconds — one breath between the impulse and the action. In Weathering Storms, you begin to extend that gap.

**The progression:**
- **Seconds:** Stop-Breathe-Name. The Stretching Branches pause. Still essential. Still the first line.
- **Minutes:** "I need to step away for a few minutes before I respond to this." Walk away. Go outside. Sit somewhere quiet. Let the first wave of the feeling crest and recede before you decide what to do.
- **Hours:** "I am going to sleep on this before I respond." The most powerful delay available. The feeling that demands immediate action at 9 PM often looks completely different at 9 AM. The overnight delay is one of the most valuable emotional regulation tools in existence.

**The rule for written or digital communication:** Never send a message written in anger. Write it if you need to — get the feeling out on paper. Then wait. Read it the next day. If you still want to send it, revise it with your thinking brain, not your feeling brain. Most of the time, you will not send it. The act of writing was the regulation. The sending would have been the damage.

**The rule for arguments:** When a conversation becomes heated — when you can feel your temperature rising and the quality of your thinking degrading — call a cooling-off. "I need to pause this conversation and come back to it later." This is not avoiding the conversation. It is saving it. Arguments conducted in emotional flooding produce damage. Arguments conducted after regulation produce resolution.

### Part 5: Managing Frustration, Anger, and Embarrassment

These are the three emotions that cause the most destruction in your age range. Not because they are bad emotions — they are not. But because their automatic action tendencies are particularly harmful.

**Frustration:**
Frustration arises when effort does not produce the expected result. You try and fail. You try again and fail again. The gap between your expectation and reality grows, and frustration fills the space.

The danger of frustration is that it convinces you to quit. Frustration says: "This is impossible. You cannot do it. Stop trying." If you obey frustration, you stop growing — because everything worth achieving involves sustained effort past the point of frustration.

**What to do with frustration:**
1. Name it: "I am frustrated."
2. Identify the gap: "I expected ___ and instead ___."
3. Assess honestly: "Is this actually impossible, or is it difficult?" (Almost always, it is difficult, not impossible.)
4. Change the approach, not the goal. Frustration means this method is not working. Try a different method.
5. If the frustration is too intense to think clearly, step away. Come back when the wave has passed.

**Anger:**
Anger arises when a boundary is crossed — when something feels unfair, when someone has wronged you, or when your sense of how things should be has been violated. Anger is the most energized emotion. It produces a powerful impulse to act — to attack, to punish, to dominate, to restore what feels like justice.

The danger of anger is that it feels righteous. Every other difficult emotion feels bad. Anger feels powerful. It feels justified. This is why people indulge anger more than other emotions — it feels good to be angry, because anger comes with a story about being right. But the actions taken in anger are almost never proportionate, almost never effective, and almost always produce consequences that outlast the feeling.

**What to do with anger:**
1. Name it: "I am angry."
2. Identify the boundary: "What was crossed? What feels unfair?"
3. Assess the interpretation: "Is my reading of this situation accurate? Am I seeing all of it?"
4. Delay. Do not act from anger. Anger can inform your response — if someone actually crossed a line, you may need to address that — but the response must come from your thinking brain, not from the anger itself.
5. Physical release helps: walk, run, do push-ups, move your body. Anger is energy. Give it a physical outlet before you give it a verbal one.

**Embarrassment:**
Embarrassment arises when your self-image is threatened — when you are seen in a way you do not want to be seen, or when you make a mistake publicly, or when you fail in front of people whose opinion matters to you. Embarrassment is the most social of the difficult emotions. It is not about what happened — it is about who saw it happen.

The danger of embarrassment is deflection. Embarrassment feels so painful that the automatic impulse is to redirect attention — usually by attacking someone else, by making a joke at someone else's expense, by lying, or by withdrawing entirely. These deflection strategies protect the ego in the moment and damage relationships and integrity over time.

**What to do with embarrassment:**
1. Name it: "I am embarrassed."
2. Tolerate it. Embarrassment is painful but not dangerous. It will pass.
3. Resist the deflection impulse. Do not attack. Do not lie. Do not make someone else the target.
4. If possible, own it. "That was embarrassing. I made a mistake." The person who can own their embarrassment publicly has more power — not less — than the person who desperately hides it.
5. Assess proportionality: "How much will this matter in a week? A month? A year?" Almost all embarrassing moments are forgotten by everyone else long before you stop replaying them.

### Part 6: Handling Disappointment Without Collapse

Disappointment is the feeling that arrives when reality falls short of expectation. You wanted something — worked for it, hoped for it, counted on it — and it did not happen. The team lost. The grade was lower than expected. The friend chose someone else. The plan fell apart.

Disappointment is painful. It is supposed to be. It is the signal that something you valued did not materialize. The pain is proportional to the value.

**The danger of disappointment is collapse.** Not the disappointment itself — the conclusions that disappointment tempts you to draw. Disappointment, left unexamined, tends to metastasize into larger stories:
- "I didn't get the grade" → "I'm not smart enough" → "I'll never be smart enough" → "Why bother trying?"
- "I got cut from the team" → "I'm not good enough" → "I was stupid to think I could make it" → withdrawal from all competition

Notice the progression: a specific event becomes a global conclusion about identity. That progression is the collapse. And it is not a necessary response to disappointment — it is a pattern that can be interrupted.

**What to do with disappointment:**
1. Feel it. Do not rush past it. The disappointment is real and it hurts and you are allowed to sit with that.
2. Keep it specific. "I did not get this thing" is a fact. "I will never get anything" is a story. The fact is true. The story is not.
3. Separate the event from your identity. Not getting the grade does not make you stupid. Not making the team does not make you worthless. The event is something that happened. You are a person to whom it happened. Those are not the same thing.
4. Ask the productive question: "What can I learn from this? What, if anything, can I do differently?"
5. Re-engage. After the feeling has passed and the reflection is done, get back in. Do not withdraw. The person who can be disappointed and return to the field has something that the person who withdraws does not.

### Part 7: Emotion Journaling — Your Pattern Book

Starting now, you keep an emotion journal. This is not a diary. It is a data collection tool — a record of your emotional life that, over time, reveals patterns you cannot see from inside a single moment.

**What to record after any significant emotional event:**

1. **Date and time**
2. **What happened** (the trigger — just the facts)
3. **What I felt** (the specific emotion or emotions, with intensity 1-10)
4. **What I thought** (the interpretation — what story did I tell myself about what happened?)
5. **What I did** (the action I took)
6. **What I wish I had done** (if different from what I actually did)
7. **What I notice** (any pattern I see — "this is the third time this week I got angry when someone corrected me")

You do not need to write a novel. A few sentences for each entry is sufficient. The value is in the accumulation — after weeks of entries, patterns emerge that are invisible in any single event.

**Review your journal weekly.** Look for recurring triggers, recurring interpretations, and recurring action patterns. Ask: "What situation keeps producing the same reaction? What is my default interpretation? What automatic action do I keep choosing?" The patterns will become clear. Once they are clear, they become changeable.

### Part 8: Cooling-Off Protocols

A cooling-off protocol is an agreed-upon procedure for pausing an interaction when emotions are escalating — before things are said or done that cannot be taken back.

**How to establish a cooling-off protocol:**
1. Discuss it in advance, when everyone is calm. Not during a conflict.
2. Agree on the signal — a word, a phrase, a gesture that means: "I need to pause this conversation. I am becoming too emotional to think clearly."
3. Agree on what happens after the signal: both people separate for a defined period (15 minutes, 30 minutes, an hour).
4. Agree that after the cooling period, the conversation resumes. The protocol is a pause, not an escape.
5. Both parties have equal right to call the protocol. No one gets to override it.

**The signal is not a weapon.** It is not "I'm calling a cooling-off because I'm winning this argument and I don't want to hear your side." It is "I am becoming unable to think clearly and I need to reset." The honesty of the signal matters. If it is abused — used to avoid rather than to regulate — it loses its power.

**Practice using the protocol in low-stakes situations first.** The first time you use it should not be in the worst fight you have ever had. Use it in mild disagreements so the habit is established before the high-pressure moment arrives.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: Emotion Journaling [Daily or After Significant Events]
Record emotional events using the seven-point format from Part 7. Review weekly for patterns. This is the most important practice in Weathering Storms — it is the tool that converts individual emotional moments into self-knowledge.

### Practice 2: Trigger Mapping [Ongoing, Reviewed Monthly]
Using data from your emotion journal, maintain a list of your known triggers. Update it as you discover new patterns. For each trigger, note: what the trigger is, what feeling it produces, what your default response is, and what response you want to develop instead.

### Practice 3: The Delayed Response [Daily, As Opportunities Arise]
When you feel the impulse to respond immediately to a provocation, practice delaying. Start with minutes. Build to hours. For anything written (text, email, online), make the overnight rule your standard. Track how often the delayed response differs from what you would have said in the moment.

### Practice 4: Post-Conflict Reflection [After Any Conflict]
After any argument, disagreement, or interpersonal conflict, write a structured reflection: What happened? What did I feel? What did I do? What was the other person's perspective? What would I do differently? This is the Stretching Branches reflection expanded with the addition of perspective-taking — considering the other person's experience, not just your own.

### Practice 5: Cooling-Off Protocol Use [As Needed]
Establish and practice cooling-off protocols with family members and, if appropriate, with close friends. Track when you use them and what happens as a result. The goal is that calling a cooling-off becomes a normal, respected practice — not a sign of weakness, but a sign of self-knowledge.

### Practice 6: Frustration Tolerance Training [Weekly]
Deliberately engage in activities that produce frustration: a difficult puzzle, a challenging physical skill, a complex problem. Practice staying with the frustration without quitting. Notice what frustration feels like in your body. Notice the quit impulse when it arrives. Continue despite it. The capacity to tolerate frustration is built through exposure, not avoidance.

### Practice 7: Weekly Emotional Review [Weekly, With Mentor]
At the end of each week, sit with your mentor for 10-15 minutes. Review the week's emotional landscape: What was the strongest emotion this week? What triggered it? How did you handle it? What patterns are you seeing? What are you working on for next week? This is the IC3.1 version of the weekly review from IC1.1 — same structure, focused on emotional self-governance.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### From Regulator to Coach

In Stretching Branches, your primary tool was your own calm presence. In Weathering Storms, your primary tool is the question.

**Instead of:**
- Naming the child's emotion for them → ask: "What are you feeling right now?"
- Telling them to pause → ask: "What do you want to do right now? Is that what you actually want to do?"
- Directing them to reflect → ask: "What happened in that interaction? Walk me through it."
- Providing the interpretation → ask: "What story were you telling yourself about what happened?"

The shift is from doing the regulation work for the student to prompting them to do it for themselves. This requires patience, because a 10-year-old's self-analysis will be crude compared to what you could offer. But crude self-analysis developed by the student is more valuable than sophisticated analysis provided by the adult — because it builds the internal capacity that will serve them when you are not present.

### Maintaining the Standard While Expanding Autonomy

The Stretching Branches standards do not relax in Weathering Storms. The student is still expected to:
- Name their emotions accurately
- Pause before acting on strong feelings
- Use words instead of outbursts
- Engage in reflection after emotional incidents

What changes is that you expect the student to do these things with decreasing prompting. By mid-Weathering Storms, the student should be initiating their own pauses, conducting their own post-event reflections (through the emotion journal), and identifying their own triggers without being told what they are.

If the student is not progressing toward this independence, the question to ask is not "Why won't they do it?" but "What am I still doing for them that they should be doing for themselves?" In many cases, the mentor's continued intervention — naming the student's emotions, calling the pause, directing the reflection — is actually preventing the transfer of ownership that Weathering Storms requires.

### Handling the Adolescent Transition

For students entering puberty during Weathering Storms (typically 10-14), expect temporary regression in emotional regulation. This is neurobiologically normal. The prefrontal cortex (regulation, planning, judgment) is undergoing massive restructuring during adolescence, while the amygdala (emotional reactivity) is fully online and newly sensitized by hormonal changes. The result is a period of heightened emotional reactivity with temporarily reduced regulation capacity.

**What to do:**
- Maintain the framework. The tools still work — they just work less reliably for a period.
- Increase patience without decreasing expectations. The standard remains the same. The timeline for meeting it extends.
- Name the biology when appropriate: "Your brain is doing a lot of restructuring right now. Strong emotions are partly biological. The tools still apply — they may just take more effort for a while."
- Do not dismiss emotional intensity as "just hormones." The emotions are real even if their intensity is partly biological. Dismissal teaches the student that their internal experience is not to be trusted, which undermines the entire IC3.1 project.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Trigger Identification

**Developing well:** The student can name their primary triggers without prompting. They show evidence of pattern recognition: "I notice that I always get angry when..." Their trigger map is maintained and updated.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot identify what triggers them. Each emotional event feels novel and unconnected to previous events. No pattern recognition is emerging.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the emotion journal being maintained? Are you conducting weekly reviews that look for patterns? Is the student resisting self-examination, or genuinely unable to see the patterns? (If the former, explore the resistance. If the latter, more data is needed — extend the journaling period.)

### 2. Feeling-Action Separation

**Developing well:** The student regularly experiences strong emotions without automatic behavioral responses. They can articulate: "I felt furious but I didn't say anything until I calmed down." The gap between feeling and action is evident and growing.

**Developing slowly:** Strong emotions still produce automatic responses. The student recognizes the pattern in retrospect but cannot interrupt it in real time.

**Note:** This develops unevenly across emotion types. A student may separate feeling from action well with frustration but not with embarrassment. Assess each emotion type separately.

### 3. Delayed Reaction

**Developing well:** The student demonstrates the ability to wait before responding — minutes or hours, not just seconds. They use cooling-off protocols. They apply the overnight rule for written communication. They can articulate why the delay mattered: "I would have said something really harsh if I had responded immediately."

**Developing slowly:** Every emotional trigger demands immediate response. The student cannot tolerate the discomfort of sitting with an unresolved feeling.

### 4. Disappointment Management

**Developing well:** The student experiences disappointment without collapsing into global conclusions about their identity or capability. They can feel the disappointment, keep it specific, and re-engage within a reasonable timeframe.

**Developing slowly:** Disappointment produces spiraling: "I failed this test" becomes "I'm stupid" becomes "nothing matters." The student withdraws after disappointments and resists re-engagement.

**How to address:** Help the student catch the spiral in real time. "You said you failed the test. That's specific. Then you said you're stupid. That's global. Are those the same thing?" Teaching the student to distinguish between a specific event and a global conclusion is one of the highest-leverage interventions in IC3.1-B.

### 5. Emotion Journal Quality

**Developing well:** Entries are honest, specific, and show deepening self-awareness over time. The student begins to notice patterns without prompting. The journal becomes a genuine tool, not a compliance exercise.

**Developing slowly:** Entries are rote, vague, or missing. "I was mad. I yelled. I shouldn't have." No specificity, no pattern recognition, no genuine engagement.

**How to improve:** Make the weekly review engaging, not bureaucratic. Celebrate pattern discoveries. Share your own patterns. If the written format is the barrier, allow voice recordings or conversations that you transcribe together.

### 6. Cooling-Off Protocol Use

**Developing well:** The student uses cooling-off protocols appropriately — calling them when emotions are escalating, respecting them when others call them, and returning to the conversation after cooling. They can articulate what the protocol did for them.

**Developing slowly:** The student does not use the protocol, uses it to avoid rather than to regulate, or refuses to respect it when others call it.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Intellectualizing Instead of Feeling
Weathering Storms introduces cognitive tools — trigger analysis, interpretation examination, pattern recognition. Some students will use these tools to avoid actually feeling the feeling. They analyze the emotion instead of experiencing it. "Interesting — that triggered my shame-anger pattern" said while the emotion is still active is not regulation. It is intellectualized avoidance. The feeling must be felt before it is analyzed. The analysis happens after, in the journal and in reflection, not instead of the experience.

### Over-Processing
The flip side of the previous pitfall: some mentor-student pairs will turn every emotional event into a 30-minute discussion. IC3.1 is not therapy. The reflection is brief, specific, and action-oriented. What happened? What did you feel? What did you do? What would you do differently? Move on. Emotional events are part of normal life, not crises that require extended processing.

### Comparing the Student to Others
"Your sister never has these kinds of outbursts." "At your age, you should be past this." Comparison undermines the entire IC3.1 project. Emotional development is individual. Assess the student against their own trajectory, not against siblings, peers, or your expectations of what their age "should" look like.

### Making the Journal a Surveillance Tool
If you read the student's journal without permission, or use their journal entries against them in arguments, you destroy the practice. The journal depends on honesty. Honesty depends on privacy. If you cannot maintain the privacy boundary, do not implement the journal.

### Confusing Regulation with Suppression
The student who never shows emotion is not regulated — they are suppressed. Regulation means feeling the emotion fully and choosing the response. Suppression means not feeling (or not showing) the emotion at all. If your student has become emotionally flat, investigate: have they learned that showing emotion is unsafe? Have they received the message that the goal is to not feel? IC3.1 explicitly rejects emotional suppression. The goal is sovereignty over feelings, not the absence of them.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Three Biggest Triggers
**Strong:** Names specific triggers with self-awareness: "Criticism from my coach, being left out of group plans, and losing at things I care about." Can describe the pattern each trigger follows and how they identified it.
**Weak:** "I don't know." "Everything bothers me." No specificity, no evidence of pattern recognition.

### Prompt 2: Delayed Reaction
**Strong:** Recalls a specific instance with detail. Describes what happened during the delay — what changed between the initial impulse and the eventual response. Shows understanding of why the delay mattered.
**Weak:** Cannot recall an instance of delayed reaction. Every reaction was immediate.

### Prompt 3: Feeling vs. Interpretation
**Strong:** Can give a real example from their own life and articulate how a different interpretation would have produced a different feeling. Shows cognitive flexibility.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish between the event and their interpretation of it. "That IS unfair" — no space between the event and the meaning they assigned to it.

### Prompt 4: Handling Disappointment
**Strong:** Honest about their actual pattern, not their ideal one. "I usually shut down for a day and then I can re-engage." Shows self-awareness about the collapse tendency and some developing capacity to interrupt it.
**Weak:** Either claims disappointment does not affect them (suppression, not regulation) or describes collapse without any sign of pattern recognition or developing management.

### Prompt 5: Journal Patterns
**Strong:** Can name a specific pattern the journal revealed, with examples. "I noticed that I get angry every time someone interrupts me, and my default is to go quiet and then bring it up passive-aggressively later." The specificity and honesty indicate genuine engagement with the practice.
**Weak:** "I haven't noticed anything." Either the journal is not being used or the reflection is not happening.

### Prompt 6: Cooling-Off Protocol
**Strong:** Describes the protocol, when they use it, and what it does for them. Can articulate a time it was difficult to call but they did anyway, or a time they wished they had called it and didn't.
**Weak:** Protocol not established, or established but never used.
